overview.title: El fin de semana con los amigos
overview.topic: Planning a weekend mountain hike with friends

notes.heading: Present tense for immediate future plans vs. future tense for deliberate intentions
notes.body: 'Voy a las montañas este fin de semana' (present) treats the plan as already a settled reality — immediate, certain, done. 'Saldremos a las seis' (future) feels more like a deliberate scheduled commitment — an itinerary. Both express future events, but the present conveys proximity and certainty while the future signals deliberate planning. Neither is more correct; the choice shifts the register.

notes.heading: Si + present indicative (open conditional) vs. si + imperfect subjunctive (contrary-to-fact)
notes.body: These two si-clause structures must not be confused. 'Si el tiempo está bueno' (present indicative) = genuinely possible, open condition. 'Si tuviéramos más tiempo' (imperfect subjunctive) = hypothetical, contrary to current fact. The result clause follows accordingly: open condition → future ('será espectacular'); contrary-to-fact → conditional ('nos quedaríamos'). Crucially, Spanish never uses the future indicative directly after si — 'si estará' is always wrong.

notes.heading: Irregular future stems
notes.body: Several common verbs have irregular future stems that must be memorized: salir → saldr-, querer → querr-, tener → tendr-, poder → podr-, saber → sabr-, hacer → har-, decir → dir-, venir → vendr-. The endings are regular (-é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án); only the stem changes. These same irregular stems are used for the conditional tense, so learning them once covers both tenses.

notes.heading: Future tense for confident predictions about others
notes.body: 'Querrán parar a descansar' uses the future not because it is planned but because it is a confident prediction about what others will want. The future tense in Spanish covers both scheduled events and speaker predictions or assumptions. This predictive use has no clean parallel in English, where 'they will want' and 'they are going to want' feel nearly identical. In Spanish, the simple future leans more toward prediction; ir a + infinitive leans more toward intention.

sentences.translation: This weekend I'm going to the mountains with my friends — something I've been eagerly looking forward to for weeks.
parts: con mis amigos a las montañas — algo que
con muchas ganas desde hace semanas.
rationale: voy → presente: ir + a destination in the present tense expresses a definite near-future plan — more immediate and certain than the simple future. 'Voy a las montañas' treats the trip as already set.
espero → presente: esperar with con ganas describes a current ongoing emotional state, not a future event. The anticipation exists right now.

sentences.translation: On Saturday morning, we'll wake up at six and head for the trail before dawn.
parts: El sábado por la mañana, nos
hacia el sendero antes del amanecer.
rationale: despertaremos → futuro simple: a planned action at a specific future time — more deliberate and scheduled-sounding than the present tense, emphasizing commitment to the plan.
saldremos → futuro simple: salir is an irregular future stem (saldr-). A second planned sequential action, reinforcing the sense of a firm itinerary.

sentences.translation: If the weather is good, the view from the summit will be spectacular.
parts: bueno, la vista desde la cima
rationale: está → presente: si + present indicative opens a real, open conditional — the weather being good is genuinely possible, not hypothetical. Spanish uses present indicative here, never future, after si in open conditionals.
será → futuro simple: the consequence of a real open condition uses the future tense — what will happen if the condition is met.

sentences.translation: No doubt, my friends will want to stop to rest, but I will prefer to keep going to the summit.
rationale: querrán → futuro simple: querer is an irregular future stem (querr-). The future here expresses a confident prediction about what others will want — not a known fact, but a strong expectation.
preferiré → futuro simple: contrasts my future preference against theirs. Two futures in the same sentence highlight competing future intentions between different subjects.

sentences.translation: If we had more time, we'd stay two whole days and make a campfire at night.
rationale: tuviéramos → subjuntivo imperfecto: si + imperfect subjunctive opens a contrary-to-fact conditional — we don't have more time, so this is purely hypothetical. Contrast with sentence 3 where si + present indicative expressed a real possibility.
nos quedaríamos → condicional: the consequence of a hypothetical si-clause uses the conditional — what we would do if circumstances were different.
haríamos → condicional: a second conditional action in the same hypothetical scenario, both contingent on the same unmet condition.

sentences.translation: For now, I just know that this weekend will be exactly what I need — fresh air, good company, and mountains.
parts: Por ahora, solo sé que este fin de semana
— aire fresco, buena compañía y montañas.
rationale: será → futuro simple: a confident prediction about an upcoming event — not a plan exactly, but an anticipation of how things will turn out.

necesito → presente: what I need is a current, immediate truth — not future, not hypothetical. The present tense grounds the sentence in the speaker's existing emotional reality, contrasting with the future será.
